title: Chapter 23: The Brother
content: The car was a silver Mercedes — rental plates, French registry — and it moved up the gravel drive with the hesitant rhythm of someone who had rehearsed this moment and was now discovering the gulf between rehearsal and reality. Jane heard it from the atelier, the crunch of wheels on stone cutting through the afternoon stillness like a blade through silk.
She moved to the window. Watched the car stop.

The man who stepped out was lean, mid-thirties at a guess, with the kind of bone structure that photographs well but looks exhausted in person. His suit was charcoal gray, beautifully cut — Savile Row, probably, or one of the Milanese houses — and it hung on him with the wrongness of borrowed armor. The lapels were too sharp for his shoulders. The color was too somber for a June afternoon. Everything about the way he dressed suggested a man who had been dressed by committee, or by a wife, or by the expectations of a world that measured men by the labels they wore rather than the lives they'd lived.

He stood beside the car for a long moment, one hand resting on the roof, the other at his side. His fingers drummed once — a nervous tic, quickly suppressed — and then he straightened his tie and walked toward the front entrance.
Jane was already moving.

She reached the foyer before he did. Through the leaded glass of the front door, she could see him approaching, his stride careful and measured, a man walking toward something he wasn't sure he wanted to find. She thought about waiting. About letting him ring the bell, letting Marie answer, letting the whole thing play out with the polite fiction of an unexpected social call.
But she didn't have that kind of time.
She opened the door before his hand reached the knocker.
"Mr. Mason."
He stopped. His eyes — brown, like Bertha's, with the same depth but none of their fire — went wide, then narrowed, then went wide again as he tried to place her face.
"I'm sorry — do I know you?"
"Not yet." Jane stepped aside, holding the door open. "I'm Jane Eyre. I work with Adèle. And you'd better come in."

He hesitated. She could see the calculations running behind his eyes — the lawyer assessing risk, the brother assessing need. Need won. He stepped past her into the foyer, and she closed the door behind him with a click that sounded, in the vast marble space, like a gavel.
"Edward is in a meeting," she said. "Twenty minutes. Maybe less."
"How do you know who I am?"
"Because you look like her. Around the eyes."

Richard Mason went very still. The kind of stillness that Jane recognized — she'd seen it in the mirror, in the days after she'd first found the encrypted drive, the moment before the world rearranged itself around a new and terrible truth.
"What do you know about my sister?"

"I know you're here about her. I know you've been trying to reach her for five years. And I know what Edward told you." Jane kept her voice level, quiet. The foyer carried sound in treacherous ways. "He said she was in a private facility in Switzerland. He showed you letters from her doctor. He told you she wasn't well enough for visitors."
Richard's jaw tightened. "How —"
"Because I've seen her." Jane held his gaze and did not blink. "She's here. In this house. In the basement."

The words landed like stones dropped into still water. Richard's face cycled through three expressions in rapid succession — shock, denial, and something rawer than either. His hand came up to his mouth, pressed hard against his lips, and for a moment Jane thought he might be sick.
"No," he said. The word was barely audible. "No, that's not — he told me — there were letters. There was a doctor. Dr. Henninger, at the —"

"There is no Dr. Henninger. There is no facility." Jane's voice was gentle now, but it did not waver. "There's a room downstairs with a lock on the outside and a woman inside it who hasn't seen the sun in longer than you want to know."

Richard turned away from her. He pressed both hands flat against the foyer wall, the marble cool beneath his palms, his shoulders shaking with the effort of containing what was rising inside him. She gave him thirty seconds. She could not give him more.
"Mr. Mason. Richard. I need you to hear me, because we don't have much time."

"I believed him." His voice cracked on the second word. He turned back to face her, and his eyes were wet, and the grief in them was so raw that it made Jane's chest ache. "He told me she was sick. He showed me letters — signed by her doctor, on letterhead, everything looked — I thought she was *safe*. I thought she was being taken care of. I thought —"

"She's surviving. But she's not safe." Jane stepped closer, close enough to smell his cologne — sandalwood and something green beneath it, the same expensive wrongness as the suit. "If you want to help her, you need to listen to me right now."
"Take me to her."
"I can't. Not yet."

"You *can't* —" The frustration in his voice was a living thing, clawing at the walls of his composure. He was a man who had spent five years believing a lie, and the guilt of that belief was eating him from the inside.

"If Edward finds out you know, he'll move her," Jane said. "He'll make her disappear — really disappear this time. Not a Swiss clinic with fabricated letters. Something permanent. Something we can't trace. Do you understand?"
Richard closed his eyes. The muscle in his jaw leaped. But he nodded.
"I need you to do something else." Jane pulled back her sleeve, checked the time, checked the hallway. Eighteen minutes. Maybe less. "You're a corporate lawyer."
His eyes opened. "How do you —"

"Bertha told me. Before you ask — yes, she talks. She remembers everything. She told me her brother Richard works in corporate law in London, that he's good at it, and that he's stubborn." The ghost of a smile crossed her face. "I'm counting on the stubborn."
"Counting on it for what?"

"Edward built his empire on fraud — supply chain manipulation, creative accounting, shell companies. I have the financial records. All of them. But they're stolen data, and any case built on stolen data can be dismantled in court." She was speaking quickly now, the words precise and urgent. "You know how to trace shell companies. You know how to build a paper trail that stands up to scrutiny. If I give you access to the digital archive, can you verify the records independently? Build a separate case that doesn't depend on what I found?"

Richard stared at her. She could see the lawyer in him engaging — the part of his mind that understood evidence, that knew how to construct a narrative from fragments, that had spent a career following money through the labyrinth of corporate structure.
"Yes," he said slowly. "But I need access to the records. The original financial filings, the incorporation documents, the —"

"I can get you access. Through the digital archive. But you have to be careful — Edward's IT monitors everything. Every login, every search query, every file opened. You'll need a reason to be in the system that doesn't raise flags."

"I could say I'm reviewing the family trust." Richard's mind was working now, the grief channeled into something harder and sharper. "Bertha's trust — Edward controls it, but I'm named as secondary counsel. It would give me legitimate grounds to access the financial records."
"Would Edward question it?"

"He might. But he'd have to explain why he objects to his brother-in-law reviewing documents he's legally entitled to see." A thin smile. "That's the thing about corporate law, Miss Eyre. The paperwork protects you, even from the people who wrote it."
Jane felt something shift in the air between them — the moment when two people who have been strangers recognize, in each other, the same resolve. It was a small thing, but it was real.
Then Richard asked the question.
He looked at her — really looked, for the first time since he'd walked through the door — and his expression changed. The lawyer receded. What replaced it was simpler, more human, and more dangerous.
"Why are you doing this?" His voice was quiet. Not suspicious — searching. "You're engaged to him. You could walk away. You could leave this house tonight and never look back. Why stay?"

Jane had asked herself this question a hundred times. In the dark hours before dawn, when the château settled into its strange, watchful silence. In the atelier, with fabric between her fingers and the weight of the encrypted drive in her pocket. In the basement, sitting across from Bertha, watching a woman who had once burned with life tend the embers of her own survival.
She had never found a simple answer. But she had found a true one.

"Because walking away doesn't free her." Jane held his gaze. "Walking away doesn't stop him from doing it again — to her, to someone else, to whoever stands between him and what he wants. I'm not here to escape, Richard. I'm here to end it."
Richard looked at her with something that might have been admiration, might have been disbelief. Perhaps both. Then the disbelief fell away, and what remained was steady and clear.
He extended his hand.
"If my sister trusts you," he said, "I trust you. What do you need me to do?"

Jane took his hand. His grip was firm — too firm, the pressure of a man trying to prove something to himself. The skin of his palm was dry and cool. She felt the calluses on his fingers, the ones that came from years of turning pages and signing documents, and beneath them the rapid, telltale pulse of a heart that was still racing from the shock of the last ten minutes.

"When you leave here, go back to London. Start with the trust documents — you'll have legitimate access. Look for payments to Cabinet Fournier. Look for any entity connected to a Swiss address that doesn't trace to a real facility. Follow the money." She released his hand. "I'll find a way to get you the rest."
"Richard."
They both turned.

Edward stood at the top of the staircase. He was perfectly still — one hand resting on the banister, the other at his side, his body arranged with the casual elegance of a man who had never in his life been surprised by anything. He wore a dark navy shirt, sleeves rolled to the forearms, and his expression was perfectly pleasant.
The temperature in the foyer dropped.

Jane felt it — not in the air, exactly, but in the space between one breath and the next. The way the shadow of the staircase fell differently. The way the stone floor seemed to conduct a cold that had nothing to do with temperature. She saw Richard feel it too, a subtle stiffening of his spine, a recalibration of the man's entire bearing as he turned to face his brother-in-law.

"What a surprise." Edward descended three steps, then stopped. Not coming down — positioning himself. The height advantage was deliberate, the way everything Edward did was deliberate. "I don't believe you called ahead."

"Spur of the moment." Richard's voice was steady. Too steady — a wall erected in a hurry, but solid enough to hold. "I was in the Loire for a conference. Thought I'd see the new collection space. Jane was kind enough to show me around."

Edward's eyes moved from Richard to Jane and back. The journey took less than a second, but Jane felt it like a searchlight — the careful, clinical assessment of a man who had spent his life reading rooms, reading people, reading the invisible currents of power that flowed through every human interaction.
He smiled.
It didn't reach his eyes.
"How wonderful," he said. "Family."

The word hung in the air between them. Edward lingered on the staircase, his gaze flickering once more to Jane — a look she couldn't decode, and that frightened her more than hostility would have — before he descended the remaining steps with deliberate slowness.
"You'll stay for dinner, of course." It was not a question.
"I'd like that," Richard said. "It's been too long."
"Yes." Edward reached the foyer floor. He clapped Richard on the shoulder — a gesture of fraternal warmth that looked exactly right and felt exactly wrong. "It has."

Jane watched the two men face each other in the afternoon light that slanted through the foyer windows, and she thought: *He knows.* Not the specifics — not yet. But Edward Rochester had built an empire on the instinct for when something was off, when a thread was loose, when a foundation had begun to crack. He couldn't know what Richard and Jane had discussed in the seventeen minutes before he appeared. But every cell in his body was telling him that the equation had changed.
Four people now. Jane. Bertha. Adèle. And Richard Mason — corporate lawyer, brother, a man who had spent five years believing a lie and was now awake and ankle-deep in fury.
Edward's instincts were screaming. And Edward Rochester, when cornered, was the most dangerous animal of all.
